data.content_ref: inline:{"claim":"The simultaneous collapse was a contingent conjuncture of heterogeneous local crises lacking unified causal structure, while Egyptian persistence reflects its distinctive socio-political ecology","mechanism":"No single mechanism; instead, independent local stressors (drought severity, palace factionalization, raiding intensity) combined stochastically. Egyptian survival was not a controlled variable outcome but a result of its geographically peripheral, ecologically buffered position within the same interactive system.","scope":{"covers":["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city-states","Egyptian persistence"],"excludes":["Minoan decline (earlier)","Mesopotamian stability","Iron Age recovery patterns"]},"forbidden":[{"case":"Demonstrable correlation between collapse timing and a single shared variable (e.g., identical tephra layer, synchronized warfare horizon) across all four regions","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"Evidence that Egyptian elite consciously adopted collapse-avoiding reforms in anticipation of shared threats","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"Archaeological demonstration that any two collapsed regions shared identical failure sequences at identical chronometric resolution","eval":"rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes":"The 'Systems Collapse' model (A2) overdetermines causality by retrofitting network theory onto patchy evidence. The archaeological record shows desynchronization: Ugarit burns c. 1190, Hattusa c. 1180, Mycenaean centers c. 1200-1150. This temporal spread exceeds what single-cause mechanisms predict."}
